name: aloo gobi dry
instructions:
Preheat the oven to 400°F and line a baking sheet with foil.

ROAST THE VEGETABLES: cut potatoes into 2-inch sticks (russet preferred) and cauliflower into small florets. Spread on the baking sheet, toss with 3 tbsp olive oil. Roast for 30 minutes, tossing once halfway through, until both have browned and slightly crisped. Set aside.

TEMPER THE SPICES AND COOK THE ONION: in a large sauté pan over medium-high heat, warm 2 tbsp olive oil. Add whole cumin seeds and cook until they turn medium brown (about 1 minute max — don't burn them). Reduce heat to medium and swirl in ground turmeric. Add the finely diced yellow onion and sauté 4-6 minutes until translucent. Add a pinch of asafetida (optional but worth it), a pinch of chili powder, and 1 tbsp julienned fresh ginger. Cook 1 more minute.

COMBINE AND FINISH: stir in the roasted potatoes and cauliflower, including any charred bits from the foil. Gently mix — don't overmix or the cauliflower falls apart. Add 1 tsp kosher salt and cook 5-6 more minutes, until everything is tender but not soggy. Remove from heat, add 1 tbsp fresh lime juice. Taste and adjust lime and salt as needed. Garnish with 1/2 cup chopped fresh cilantro (stems and leaves both).
notes:
Priya krishna's aloo gobi. Different shape than tomato-based indian curries — this is a roasted-vegetable + tempered-oil-and-spice technique, not a simmered curry. Notably no tomato, no garam masala, no garlic.

WHOLE CUMIN SEEDS, NOT GROUND: the cumin is tempered whole in hot oil at the start. Ground cumin won't substitute here — the technique depends on the whole seeds blooming in the fat. If your spice rack only has ground cumin, this isn't the recipe to make.

ROAST FIRST, THEN COMBINE: the potatoes and cauliflower are roasted first (dry-heat browning), then folded into the tempered onion-and-spice base. This is what distinguishes the dish from a curry — you keep the dry-roasted edges of the vegetables rather than simmering them into softness.

ASAFETIDA: optional but really great. A pinch goes a long way — it adds the savory-funky depth that ground cumin alone won't deliver, and is what makes the dish read distinctly indian rather than just spiced.

LIME IS NON-OPTIONAL: the lime juice at the end is what brightens the whole dish. Without it, aloo gobi reads heavy and brown. Adjust salt and lime together at the end — they balance each other.

DON'T OVERMIX AT THE END: once you fold the roasted vegetables in, gentle mixing only. Aggressive stirring breaks the cauliflower florets apart and you lose the textural distinction.
